repo.description: Vim-fork focused on extensibility and usability
repo.name: neovim

comment_data.body:
## zizmor / 

credential persistence through GitHub Actions artifacts: does not set persist-credentials: false

[Show more details](https://github.com/neovim/neovim/security/code-scanning/85)
comment_data.diff_hunk:
@@ -24,6 +24,21 @@
   INSTALL_PREFIX: ${{ github.workspace }}/nvim-install
 
 jobs:
+  wasmtime:
+    strategy:
+      fail-fast: false
+      matrix:
+        test: [ubuntu-latest, macos-latest, windows-latest]
+    runs-on: ${{ matrix.test }}
+    steps:
+      - uses: actions/checkout@v5

comment_data.body: You should also run the tests here (which means this might not be the best file for it?)
comment_data.diff_hunk:
@@ -24,6 +24,23 @@ env:
   INSTALL_PREFIX: ${{ github.workspace }}/nvim-install
 
 jobs:
+  wasmtime:
+    strategy:
+      fail-fast: false
+      matrix:
+        test: [ubuntu-latest, macos-latest, windows-latest]
+    runs-on: ${{ matrix.test }}
+    steps:
+      - uses: actions/checkout@v6.0.3
+        with:
+          persist-credentials: false
+      - uses: ./.github/actions/setup
+      - run: |
+          cmake -S cmake.deps --preset ci -D ENABLE_WASMTIME=ON
+          cmake --build .deps
+          cmake --preset ci -D ENABLE_WASMTIME=ON
+          cmake --build build

comment_data.body: Note: Tracking this here (still) requires manual updating this whenever we bump the Lua parser (which is done via `scripts/bump-deps.lua`.
should we add an item to: https://github.com/neovim/neovim/blob/b153cbbb75a272e503b9e66139ccba24347ea6d9/MAINTAIN.md?plain=1#L119

N/A prefix will put this in the 1-line summary, instead of an indivisual SKIPPED line (avoids some noise in the ci report)

```suggestion
      'N/A ENABLE_WASMTIME not enabled'
```
comment_data.diff_hunk:
@@ -0,0 +1,85 @@
+local t = require('test.testutil')
+local n = require('test.functional.testnvim')()
+
+local clear = n.clear
+local command = n.command
+local eq = t.eq
+local exec_lua = n.exec_lua
+local insert = n.insert
+local skip = t.skip
+
+local lua_text = [[
+local M = {}
+
+-- returns the sum of two numbers
+local function add(a, b)
+  return a + b
+end
+
+M.add = add
+return M
+]]
+
+describe('treesitter WASM parser', function()
+  before_each(clear)
+
+  it('lua WASM parser produces same highlight captures as native parser', function()
+    skip(
+      exec_lua('return vim._ts_add_language_from_wasm == nil'),
+      'Neovim not built with ENABLE_WASMTIME'

comment_data.body: Should this be an error? seems weird to ~silently skip after we just checked `vim._ts_add_language_from_wasm` above

+--- @param repo string
+--- @param ref string
+--- @param filename string
+local function get_release_artifact_info(repo, ref, filename)
+  local temp_dir = os.getenv('TMPDIR') or os.getenv('TEMP') or '/tmp'
+
+  local artifact_path = temp_dir .. '/' .. filename
+  local artifact_url = 'https://github.com/'
+    .. repo
+    .. '/releases/download/'
+    .. ref
+    .. '/'
+    .. filename
+
+  run_die(
+    { 'curl', '-sfL', artifact_url, '-o', artifact_path },
+    'Failed to download release artifact from GitHub'
+  )
+
+  local shacmd = (
+    vim.fn.executable('sha256sum') == 1 and { 'sha256sum', artifact_path }
+    or { 'shasum', '-a', '256', artifact_path }
+  )
+  local artifact_sha = run(shacmd):gmatch('%w+')()

comment_data.body: FYI, this script is Linux-only in its current form (doesn't even work properly on macos).

comment_regular_data.body:
Point 2 has in fact changed: wasmtime now makes LTS releases on a yearly cycle, which I switched to tracking in tree-sitter.

I think it would still make sense to exercise this in a separate optional workflow (a test that isn't run doesn't exist ;))
I can revert the workflow changes from ed9f682576dffa5c1d54fb4f01fbdd3c82ea2c06, but I'm not sure what "separate optional workflow" means.
That's exactly what I mean :) (Not enabling wasmtime for all test workflows but add a new, optional one.)

Yeah, it's not as simple as a revert. How should I proceed with running the tests?

- Should I add ENABLE_WASMTIME as a matrix dimension on the test workflow? That means 3 new jobs (ubuntu/darwin/windows) on every pull request, but it's the safest.
- Should I always ENABLE_WASMTIME for test jobs? Appears to approximately double the build time. I need to verify that this also wouldn't affect the published nightlies/releases.
- If not blanket-setting ENABLE_WASMTIME, should I filter the job spec on wasm/tree-sitter related paths? That could reduce resource usage but runs the risk of silently breaking.
- If not blanket-setting ENABLE_WASMTIME, should I run the full test suite or only wasm/tree-sitter related tests? Same considerations.

I think what @clason was suggesting (correct me if wrong), is a separate `.github/workflows/test_wasm.yml` . 

For now it can just check linux. When it graduates later, then we can add it back to the matrix in `test.yml` , maybe

I'll let others weigh in on the exact workflow shape (especially @dundargoc), but
1. wasmtime should **not** be part of the general test matrix,
2. there should be an optional (for now) separate workflow that builds with wasmtime and runs just the wasm test (and possibly all treesitter specs, but I don't think that will be necessary),
3. we don't need full architecture coverage (although, again, eventually we will want to) -- for now it's enough to just test Linux (and/or macOS).

The point is simply to catch when any change to the treesitter stack (like a tree-sitter bump) breaks wasm support (again). This is important to project confidence for people wishing to opt-in and test-drive this feature. (It also gives us a validation target for switching to a different wasm runtime, should we wish to.)
This version appears to do what you're asking for (it's a separate workflow running just the wasm test on ubuntu).
Made the requested changes to bump_deps in for both the function I added and the function I copied.
